text: In a PPP lesson, when do students typically use language freely and creatively?
hint: C1: Production stage – freer, more creative use of target language.

text: What is the purpose of concept checking questions (CCQs)?
options: To check students' understanding of meaning without using the target language
hint: C1: CCQs use simple language to check comprehension of new concepts.

text: Which is an example of a closed task?
options: Write a story about your weekend
Discuss your opinion on climate change
Complete the sentence with the correct word
hint: C1: Closed tasks have one correct answer; open tasks have multiple possibilities.

text: What does a timeline help to teach?
hint: C1: Timelines visually represent time relationships between actions.

text: Which is the best way to give instructions for a complex task?
options: Read the instructions aloud once quickly
Give step-by-step instructions, demonstrate, and check understanding
Write instructions on the board and ignore questions
hint: C1: ICQs (Instruction Checking Questions) ensure understanding.
C1: Drilling includes choral and individual repetition for accuracy.

text: Which is an example of error correction that promotes self-correction?
options: The teacher says the correct form immediately
The teacher says 'Not correct' and moves on
The teacher repeats the error with rising intonation and pauses
hint: C1: Echoing with rising intonation signals an error without giving the answer.

options: Students learn grammar rules first, then practice
Students complete a meaningful task using language, then focus on form
hint: C1: TBL framework: pre-task → task cycle → language focus.

text: Which is a feature of a teacher-centered classroom?
options: Students work in groups most of the time
The teacher talks most of the lesson
hint: C1: Teacher-centered = transmission model; learner-centered = construction model.

options: Asking students to repeat after the teacher
Drawing out language or ideas from students rather than giving them
hint: C1: Elicitation activates prior knowledge and increases student involvement.

options: A detailed outline of what will happen in a lesson
hint: C1: Lesson plans include objectives, procedures, materials, timing, and assessment.

options: The overall goal of the entire lesson
The purpose of a specific part of the lesson
hint: C1: Stage aims help sequence and justify activities.

options: A goal for the teacher's own development
hint: C1: Personal aims focus on improving teaching skills (e.g., reduce TTT).

text: What does TTT stand for in CELTA methodology?
hint: C1: Reducing TTT increases STT (Student Talking Time).

options: An activity at the end of a lesson
An activity at the beginning to engage students and activate prior knowledge
hint: C1: Warm-ups transition students into the lesson and create interest.

options: An activity that introduces the topic
An activity that ends the lesson
hint: C1: Lead-ins create context and prepare learners for the main task.

options: An activity at the beginning of a lesson
An activity at the end to consolidate and end positively
hint: C1: Coolers review learning and leave a positive final impression.

options: A short activity used when there is extra time
The main activity of the lesson
hint: C1: Fillers are flexible, low-prep, and engaging.

options: An activity prepared in case the main activity fails
hint: C1: Back-ups ensure the lesson doesn't fail due to technical or engagement issues.

text: What is the difference between a worksheet and a handout?
options: Worksheet has tasks to complete; handout has information only
Worksheet is digital; handout is paper
Worksheet is for homework; handout is for class
hint: C1: Handouts provide reference; worksheets require active response.

options: Any image, chart, or object used to support teaching
hint: C1: Visual aids support comprehension, memory, and engagement.

options: Real-life objects brought to the classroom for teaching
hint: C1: Realia makes learning concrete and memorable.

options: A card with a word or picture used for drilling vocabulary
hint: C1: Flashcards support vocabulary, grammar, and quick recall activities.

options: Students walk around and talk to multiple partners
hint: C1: Mingles maximize student interaction and speaking practice.

options: Each student has part of the information; they must share to complete the task
hint: C1: Jigsaws promote cooperation and information-gap communication.

options: Students act out imaginary situations in assigned roles
Students read aloud from a textbook
hint: C1: Role-plays develop pragmatic competence and fluency in realistic contexts.

options: An extended, realistic role-play with complex scenarios
hint: C1: Simulations (e.g., business meeting) involve multiple skills and preparation.

options: A structured argument where students defend opposing positions
hint: C1: Debates develop critical thinking, persuasion, and formal language.

options: Students write down what the teacher reads aloud
hint: C1: Dictation develops listening, spelling, and punctuation.

options: Students run to a text, remember a phrase, and dictate to a partner
hint: C1: Running dictation combines kinesthetic, reading, writing, and memory skills.

options: A text with words removed for students to fill in
hint: C1: Cloze tests measure reading comprehension and grammar knowledge.

options: A sentence or text with missing words to complete
hint: C1: Gap-fills can be open (no options) or closed (word bank).

options: A question with several possible answers, one correct
hint: C1: MCQs are efficient for testing recognition knowledge.
C1: Peer assessment develops critical thinking and collaboration.
C1: Self-assessment promotes metacognition and learner autonomy.

options: A collection of student work demonstrating progress
hint: C1: Portfolios show development over time and multiple skills.

options: Adapting teaching to meet diverse learner needs
Teaching all students the same way
hint: C1: Differentiation includes content, process, product, and environment.

options: A preferred way of learning (visual, auditory, kinesthetic, etc.)
hint: C1: Addressing learning styles increases engagement and retention.
C1: VAK is a learning style model, though research is mixed.

options: Motivation from external rewards (grades, praise, money)
hint: C1: Intrinsic motivation leads to deeper, more durable learning.

options: Motivation from external rewards or consequences
hint: C1: Extrinsic motivation can be effective but less sustainable.

options: The techniques teachers use to maintain order and engagement
hint: C1: Good management maximizes learning time and minimizes disruption.

options: An arrangement of where students sit in the classroom
hint: C1: Seating plans can manage behavior and support pair/group work.

text: What is a code of conduct?
options: A set of rules for behavior in the classroom
hint: C1: Clear codes of conduct prevent problems and ensure fairness.

options: A reward given to increase desired behavior
A punishment to decrease undesired behavior
hint: C1: Praise, stickers, and privileges are positive reinforcers.
C1: Sanctions should be proportionate, consistent, and educative.

text: What is a safe learning environment?
options: A classroom where students feel physically and emotionally secure
hint: C1: Safety includes freedom from bullying, ridicule, and discrimination.

options: A classroom where all students, regardless of ability, learn together
A classroom for advanced students only
hint: C1: Inclusion requires differentiation, accommodation, and respect.

text: What is a special educational need (SEN)?
options: A learning difficulty or disability requiring additional support
hint: C1: SEN includes dyslexia, ADHD, autism, and other conditions.

text: What is a gifted and talented learner?
options: A student with exceptional ability in one or more areas
hint: C1: Gifted learners need enrichment, extension, and challenge.

text: What is the difference between accuracy and fluency?
options: Accuracy is about correct form; fluency is about smooth communication
Accuracy is about speed; fluency is about correctness
Accuracy is for writing; fluency is for speaking
Accuracy is for beginners; fluency is for advanced
hint: C1: Both are important; different stages focus on different goals.

text: What is a diagnostic test used for?
options: To identify learners' strengths and weaknesses before a course
To compare learners to each other
hint: C1: Diagnostic assessment informs planning and differentiation.

text: What is a placement test used for?
options: To assign learners to appropriate levels or classes
To measure learning at the end of a course
hint: C1: Placement tests ensure students are in suitable groups.

text: What is a progress test used for?
options: To check what has been learned during a course
To measure learning at the end of a course
hint: C1: Progress tests are formative and occur during instruction.

text: What is an achievement test used for?
options: To measure what has been learned at the end of a course
To place students at the beginning
hint: C1: Achievement tests are summative (final exams, unit tests).

text: What is a proficiency test used for?
options: To measure general language ability regardless of course content
hint: C1: Proficiency tests (IELTS, TOEFL) are not based on a specific syllabus.

options: To identify what learners need to learn
To test learners at the end of a course
hint: C1: Needs analysis informs course design and materials selection.

options: A statement of what learners will be able to do after a lesson
A list of activities for the lesson
hint: C1: Objectives should be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, time-bound.

text: What is the difference between aim and procedure?
options: Aim is why; procedure is how
Aim is how; procedure is why
Aim is the activity; procedure is the goal
hint: C1: Aim = purpose; procedure = step-by-step actions.

text: What is the difference between deductive and inductive teaching?
options: Deductive: rule → examples; Inductive: examples → rule
Deductive: examples → rule; Inductive: rule → examples
Deductive is for grammar; inductive is for vocabulary
hint: C1: Deductive = teacher-centered; inductive = learner-centered.

options: Teaching through explicit grammar rules and translation exercises
hint: C1: GTM focuses on reading, writing, and L1-L2 translation.

options: Teaching exclusively in the target language, no translation
hint: C1: Direct method uses demonstration, questioning, and correction.

options: Teaching through repetition, drills, and habit formation
hint: C1: Audiolingualism emphasizes mimicry, memorization, and error prevention.

options: Teaching focused on real communication and meaning rather than form
hint: C1: CLT emphasizes fluency, authenticity, and learner interaction.

options: Teaching language through physical movement and commands
hint: C1: TPR is effective for beginners and kinesthetic learners.

options: Teacher remains silent, students discover language through cues
hint: C2: Gattegno's method uses color charts and rods.

options: Teaching through music, relaxation, and positive suggestion
hint: C2: Lozanov's method reduces anxiety and uses Baroque music.

options: Teaching focuses on chunks, collocations, and formulaic language
hint: C2: Lewis: language is lexical, not grammatical.

options: A large collection of authentic language texts for analysis
hint: C2: Corpora inform lexicography and teaching materials.

options: A line from a corpus showing a word in context
hint: C2: Concordances help learners discover patterns.

options: Words that frequently occur together (make a decision, heavy rain)
hint: C1: Collocations are a key focus of the lexical approach.

options: A multi-word unit stored as a whole (How are you?, I don't know)
hint: C1: Chunks include collocations, idioms, and discourse markers.

options: A fixed phrase used for specific functions (Nice to meet you, See you later)
hint: C1: Formulaic expressions are learned as wholes, not analyzed.

text: What is the difference between a mistake and an error?
options: Mistake is performance-based (can self-correct); error is competence-based
Error is performance-based; mistake is competence-based
Mistakes are serious; errors are minor
hint: C1: Errors reflect gaps in knowledge; mistakes are slips.

options: When errors become permanent despite continued exposure and instruction
hint: C2: Fossilization is common in pronunciation and grammar.

options: The learner's developing language system between L1 and L2
hint: C1: Interlanguage is systematic, dynamic, and has its own rules.

options: When the learner's first language affects L2 production (negative transfer)
When the learner uses only L1
When the textbook is in L1
hint: C1: Interference can be phonological, lexical, or grammatical.

options: When L1 helps L2 learning (similar structures)
hint: C1: Cognates and similar grammar rules facilitate positive transfer.

options: Applying a rule too broadly (goed instead of went)
hint: C1: Overgeneralization is a common developmental error.

options: Using simpler structures than needed (no third person -s)
hint: C1: Simplification is a natural stage in interlanguage development.

text: What is the order of acquisition?
options: Grammar features are acquired in a predictable sequence across learners
All learners acquire grammar at the same time
hint: C2: -ing before past tense, plural -s before third person -s.

text: What is the difference between learning and acquisition?
options: Acquisition is subconscious; learning is conscious rule knowledge
Acquisition is conscious; learning is subconscious
Acquisition is for children; learning is for adults
hint: C2: Krashen's acquisition-learning distinction.

options: Learners must notice a language feature to acquire it
hint: C2: Schmidt: noticing is necessary but not sufficient for acquisition.

options: Language acquisition is enhanced through conversation and negotiation of meaning
hint: C2: Long's interaction hypothesis emphasizes modified interaction.

options: Producing language (speaking/writing) is necessary for acquisition
Grammar rules must be taught explicitly
hint: C2: Swain's output hypothesis claims output forces syntactic processing.

options: Learners acquire language by understanding input slightly above their level (i+1)
hint: C2: Krashen's input hypothesis emphasizes comprehensible input.

text: What is the affective filter hypothesis?
options: Anxiety, low confidence, and motivation block language acquisition
hint: C2: Krashen: low anxiety, high motivation lower the filter.

options: Learners use conscious grammar rules to edit output after it is produced
hint: C2: Krashen: monitor use requires time, focus on form, and rule knowledge.

text: What is the natural order hypothesis?
options: Grammar rules are acquired in a predictable sequence
Grammar can be taught in any order
All learners acquire at the same speed
hint: C2: Krashen: e.g., -ing before past tense.

options: Learning occurs through social interaction and scaffolding
hint: C2: Vygotsky: ZPD (Zone of Proximal Development) and mediation.

text: What is the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD)?
options: The gap between what a learner can do alone and with help
hint: C2: Vygotsky: teaching should target the ZPD.

options: Temporary support provided to help a learner complete a task
hint: C1: Scaffolding is gradually removed as competence increases.

options: Content and Language Integrated Learning – teaching subject content in L2
hint: C2: CLIL teaches both content (e.g., science) and language.

options: Students learn content at home and practice in class
hint: C2: Flipped classrooms use videos or readings for pre-class input.
C1: Blended learning offers flexibility and variety.

text: What is a virtual learning environment (VLE)?
options: An online platform for course materials, assignments, and communication
hint: C1: Examples: Moodle, Google Classroom, Canvas.

text: What is a needs analysis in ESP?
options: Identifying language needs for specific professional or academic contexts
hint: C2: ESP (English for Specific Purposes) is needs-driven.

options: English for Academic Purposes (writing essays, lectures, reading papers)
hint: C2: EAP focuses on skills for university study.

options: An essay given at the start to identify writing strengths and weaknesses
hint: C1: Diagnostic essays inform instruction and feedback.

text: What is an observation task in CELTA?
options: A structured form for observing and analyzing lessons
hint: C1: Trainees observe experienced teachers using focus tasks.
